title: Question 8.3
statement_text: For which sets $\mathcal{S}$ of strings is it true that there is a constant $c=c(\mathcal{S})\in\mathbb{N}$ such that every $c$-padded string $S$ contains either a string in $\mathcal{S}$ or the reverse of a string in $\mathcal{S}$ as a consecutive substring?
context_text: This question arises in the special case where every graph in $\mathcal{H}$ is a $c'$-tassel for some $c'\in\mathbb{N}$, where each tassel has a unique string $\mathcal{S}_{H,v}$ associated to each neck $v$. A set $\mathcal{S}$ satisfying the condition is called a $c$-unavoidable language, and identifying minimal such languages is key to characterising tasselled families. The only singleton $c$-unavoidable languages are $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 01\}$ (up to reversal) and $\mathcal{S}=\{0\cdots 0\}$.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIII. Basic obstructions in $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs for finite $\mathcal{H}$

title: Question 8.4
statement_text: For which sets $\mathcal{S}$ of strings is it true that every sufficiently long string contains a string in $\mathcal{S}$ as a consecutive substring?
context_text: These sets $\mathcal{S}$ are the classical unavoidable languages; it is known (from [4,5]) that every minimal unavoidable language is finite. This finiteness fails for $c$-unavoidable languages (e.g., $\mathcal{S}=\{01^{k}0:k\in\mathbb{N}\}$ is $1$-unavoidable), and the paper poses the question of which results on unavoidable languages generalise to the $c$-unavoidable setting introduced in Question 8.3.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions XIII. Basic obstructions in $\mathcal{H}$-free graphs for finite $\mathcal{H}$
